tip: Feet flat on the floor. Not crossed. Not tucked under you. Flat.
Sit back in the chair. Your lower back should touch the backrest. If you're perched on the edge you're already losing.
Knees at 90 degrees, level with or slightly below your hips. If they're higher than your hips, your chair is too low.
Screen at eye level. Top of the monitor should sit at or just below your eye line. Looking down all day is how your neck stages a mutiny.
Monitor 20–28 inches from your face. Arm's length is a good quick test. If you're squinting and leaning in, move it closer.
Shoulders down. Right now. They're up near your ears, aren't they.
Elbows at 90 degrees, close to your body. If your arms are reaching forward to type, your keyboard is too far away.
Wrists neutral. Not bent up, not bent down. The keyboard should meet your hands, not the other way around.
Head over your shoulders — not jutting forward. Every inch your head drifts forward adds about 10 lbs of effective load on your neck. Do the math on 8 hours.
Lower back check: there should be a natural curve there, not a flattened slab against the chair. Lumbar support exists for a reason. Use it.
Armrests: your forearms should rest parallel to the floor when your elbows are at 90 degrees. If you're shrugging to use them, lower them. If they're useless beside your body, raise them.
Laptop users: your screen is always too low. Your neck is bent 15–30 degrees forward right now. Get a stand and an external keyboard. One purchase. Permanent fix.
Crossing your legs feels fine right now. A habit builds hip asymmetry over months. Uncross.
Chin tucks: pull your chin straight back like you're making a double chin. Hold 5 seconds, repeat 10 times. It's not glamorous but it works.
Slow neck rolls. Each direction, controlled. If something crunches, that's your body filing a complaint.
Shoulder rolls. 10 backward, 10 forward. Backward are more important — they undo what forward-hunching does all day.
Chest opener: clasp your hands behind your back, squeeze your shoulder blades together, hold 10 seconds. Feel everything in front of your chest stretch.
Upper trap stretch: tilt your ear toward your shoulder, hold 20–30 seconds. Other side. Don't force it. This muscle is probably knotted right now.
Thoracic extension: put your hands behind your head and gently arch back over the top of your chair. You're trying to get the middle of your spine to move. Stay there 10 seconds.
Wrist flexors: arm out in front, palm up, use your other hand to gently pull the fingers down toward the floor. Hold 20 seconds. Switch.
Wrist extensors: arm out, palm down, other hand gently pulls the fingers down. Different stretch, same 20 seconds. Both directions matter.
Seated spinal twist: sit tall, rotate toward one side of the chair back, put a hand on it for leverage. Hold 15–20 seconds. Other side. Your spine is not meant to be stationary all day.
Hip flexor release: sit at the edge of your chair, extend one leg back behind you with the foot on the floor. Feel the front of that hip open up. Hold 20 seconds. Switch.

Scalene stretch: tilt your ear to your shoulder, then rotate your chin slightly down toward your armpit. Hold 20 seconds. This one hits the neck muscles that pull your head forward — different from the trap stretch and probably more needed.
Levator scapulae: ear toward shoulder, then look down toward the floor on the same side. That dull ache in the corner between your neck and shoulder? That's this muscle. Hold 30 seconds.
Prayer stretch: press your palms together in front of your chest, then slowly lower your hands toward your lap until you feel the stretch across both wrists and forearms. Hold 20 seconds.
Stand up. Walk somewhere that isn't your desk. You've been sitting long enough.
10 standing calf raises while you're up. Calves are the second heart — they pump blood back up from your legs. They've been doing nothing for a while.
Wall angel: stand with your back against a wall, arms in goal-post position, press everything flat. Slowly raise your arms overhead and bring them back down. 10 reps.
Doorway stretch: stand in a doorframe, arms at 90 degrees on the frame, lean your body forward gently. Chest and shoulders. Hold 20 seconds.
Standing figure-4: cross one ankle over the opposite knee, sit back into a shallow squat. Hold 20–30 seconds. Switch legs. Your hips will thank you and stop sending complaints to your lower back.
Walk to get water. Then drink it. Hydration and movement in one trip.
5 slow bodyweight squats. Your hip flexors are shortened from sitting. These help undo that.
Take the long way back to your desk. The work will still be there.
Desk pushups: hands on the edge of your desk, shoulder-width apart, lean in and push back. 10 reps. Not impressive. Effective.
March in place for 30 seconds, knees up. Your hip flexors have been shortened into a sitting position for hours. This is them getting a small apology.
Roll your ankles. Both feet, both directions, 10 each. Sitting cuts circulation to your lower legs and ankles quietly stiffen up without announcing it.
If you have a standing desk and it's currently in sitting position, raise it. That's the entire reason you own it.
20-20-20 rule: look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds. Do it now. Your eyes have been locked at the same focal distance for a long time and they're exhausted.
Jaw check: unclench. Drop the jaw slightly, let the tongue rest on the roof of the mouth. You've been grinding.
Forehead check: consciously relax the muscles in your forehead. You were furrowing. Everyone is always furrowing.
Breathing reset: 4 counts in, hold for 4, out for 4. Do it three times. Resets the nervous system. Actually works.
Blink deliberately 10 times right now. You've been staring and your blink rate drops to almost nothing. Eyes dry out. Blink.
Palm your eyes: rub your hands together until warm, cup them gently over closed eyes. 30 seconds of darkness. Tension lives around your eyes and this dissolves some of it.
Screen brightness: it should roughly match the ambient light in the room. If it's blazing white in a dim office, your eyes are doing extra work all day.
Text size check: if you're leaning toward the screen to read, the font is too small. Zoom in. Ctrl+scroll. Your monitor doesn't have an ego.
If it's past 4 PM and you haven't turned on Night Mode or a warm color temperature, do it now. Your sleep window opens in a few hours and blue light is already messing with it.
Body scan: shoulders, jaw, hands, lower back. Find one thing that's holding tension. Just one. Let it go. That's enough for now.
Your breathing has probably been shallow for the last hour. One slow breath, deep into the belly. Let your stomach push out on the inhale. Exhale fully. Done.
